paragraphs.text: Per CHFA's 2023 Round One award narrative, no family-property LIHTC project had been built in Rifle since 2002. Rifle Apartments (60 units; 30 1-BR, 20 2-BR, 10 3-BR at 30%-80% AMI, with 14 project-based vouchers at or below 30% AMI from the county housing authority) is the city's first family LIHTC in roughly 20 years.
Groundbreaking was September 27, 2024. The grand opening was held April 17, 2026, with 95% lease-up at opening.
CHFA's 2026 Round One Housing Tax Credit announcement (May 21, 2026) does not list any Rifle development among the 14 awardees.

RHA was established in 1979 to provide affordable rental housing for senior and disabled residents. The agency operates approximately 106 low-income apartments — 60 HUD rental-assistance / Section 8 units and 46 affordable units — with roughly 150 households on the waitlist per recent reporting.

Rifle's documented direct financial commitment to the Rifle Apartments project was $600,000 (CHFA narrative §2d). The city also signed a Letter of Support endorsing the project, and supported the developer's HB 22-1304 Infrastructure & Strong Communities funding request to DOLA ($5,000,000) and DOH Gap Funds request ($3,000,000).

Insurance costs have become an intensifying pressure on affordable-housing operations nationwide. Enterprise Community Partners' February 2026 report “Curbing the Insurance Spiral” found that 29 percent of housing providers experienced property-insurance premium increases of 25 percent or higher in 2023 — many seeing jumps of 30 to 100 percent or more — on the heels of 25 consecutive quarters of rising property-insurance costs for housing providers.

Rifle has two directly verified recent development signals: Rifle Apartments opened as a 60-unit affordable rental community near downtown and was reported 95% leased, while Habitat for Humanity of the Roaring Fork Valley's modular-home facility in Rifle is planned to produce up to 200 homes a year, hire 64 employees, and train up to 100 students annually.

The Affordable Ownership Need screening module recommends Ownership-supportive strategy for City of Rifle. It classifies rental pressure as Moderate, ownership pressure as High, and identifies 526 moderate-income renter households as the ownership-fit base.

The same screening run counts 331 renter households and 638 owner households as cost-burdened. This is a screening estimate only; verify local prices, financing assumptions, assistance programs, household size, and local deed-restriction policy before using it for a project decision.

The metric digest ranks City of Rifle at #263 with an overall housing-need score of 44.8. It identifies 165 deeply affordable units missing at <=30% AMI, equal to 48.5% of <=30% AMI households. 39.3% of renter households are cost burdened, and 1.9% of occupied households are overcrowded.
Market and supply context: the digest reports a ZHVI market estimate of $413,500 and median gross rent of $1,308. 33.6% of households rent, and 14.6% of the housing stock is multifamily.

Demographics and demand: the population metric is 10,570. LEHD-based commuting signals show 2,545 in-commuters and a commute ratio of 71.6%. The digest's county-context projection shows 1,045 future units needed over 20 years, so that projection should be read as county context rather than a place trend.
Opportunity context: the digest gives an opportunity score of 44.8 and a walkability score of 21.0. These are current digest levels, not trends.

Economic and service-worker context: the digest reports a workforce-housing pressure score of 44.6. A worker earning the containing county's LEHD earnings-bin wage estimate faces an ownership affordability gap of $45,810 and a rent affordability gap of $-2,680. The county-context service-sector employment share is 36.4%, and county-context ACS cohorts show median gross rent changed 84.4% from 2009 to 2024 while median household income changed 47.1%. These county-context figures are economic context, not place-level sector or wage observations.
